products.name: Essentials Kit
products.tagline: Essentials Kit With Most Common Modules!
products.description:
The **starter kit for DUELink** — a hand-picked collection of the most common modules, plus everything you need to wire them up, mount them, and store them. Plug the included USB Hook into a PC and start sending text commands in [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, Arduino, or MicroPython. **Kit contents:** - [TFT N18 1.8" color display](/docs/products/dstftn18-b) - [LED R16 16-LED ring](/docs/products/ldledr16-b) - [Rotary encoder](/docs/products/hirotary-c) - [Ultrasonic distance sensor](/docs/products/sedistance-b) - [Servo X1 motor](/docs/products/acservox1-a) - [Buzzer](/docs/products/sobuzzer-b) - [Light sensor](/docs/products/selight-a) - [USB Hook](/docs/products/adusbhook-a) — USB-C connection to your host - Temperature sensor — built into every DUELink module's core chip - [Holey Board](/docs/products/axholey) — for mounting modules - [Mounting Hardware Variety Pack](/docs/products/axmnt) — standoffs, screws, nuts - [Cable Variety Pack](/docs/products/axcbl) — 10 cables of varying lengths - USB-C cable - Storage box
![Storage box with kit](/img/catalog/ktessen-a-6.webp)
Modules mounted on the Holey Board fit inside the included storage box for clean transport. For more sensors plus wireless, see the [Gimme More Kit](/docs/products/ktgimme-a). The two kits are designed to combine.

products.name: Gimme More Kit
products.tagline: Gimme More Modules, with Wireless Options!
products.description:
Extends the [Essentials Kit](/docs/products/ktessen-a) — or stands alone if you'd rather start here — with a different mix of modules, plus a **Wireless ESP32** for Bluetooth and Wi-Fi control. Plug the included USB Stick into a PC and control everything in [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, Arduino, or MicroPython. **Kit contents:** - [Wireless ESP32](/docs/products/wlesp32-a) — Bluetooth + Wi-Fi - [7-button keypad with LEDs](/docs/products/hibuttons7-a) - [12×8 LED matrix](/docs/products/ldmt1208-a) - [Fan motor](/docs/products/acfan-b) - [3-axis accelerometer](/docs/products/seaccel-a) - [Retro Sound 3.5 mm audio jack](/docs/products/soretrosnd-a) - [USB Stick](/docs/products/adusbstick-a) — direct USB-A connection - Temperature sensor — built into every DUELink module's core chip - [Holey Board](/docs/products/axholey) — for mounting modules - [Mounting Hardware Variety Pack](/docs/products/axmnt) - [Cable Variety Pack](/docs/products/axcbl) — 10 cables - Storage box
![Storage box with kit](/img/catalog/ktgimme-a-6.webp)
Modules mounted on the Holey Board fit inside the included storage box for clean transport. :::note No USB cable is included — USB Stick plugs directly into a USB-A port. Use a standard USB extension cable if you need length. :::

products.name: Classroom CincoBit Kit
products.tagline: A low-cost Classroom STEM kit with cables
products.description:
A **classroom STEM bundle** built around the [CincoBit](/docs/products/mccincobit-e) educational board — 14 boards in a single kit, **for under $100**. Enough to equip a typical classroom with one CincoBit per pair of students.

**Kit contents:**

- **14× [CincoBit](/docs/products/mccincobit-e) boards** — each with 5×5 LED matrix, light and temperature sensors, two buttons, and on-board buzzer
- 14× USB cables — one per board
- 2× storage boxes for organized transport between classrooms

Boards ship with [MicroBlocks](/docs/language/microblocks) firmware ready to go, and a complete [middle-school CS curriculum](/docs/tutorial/cincobit) is available — no extra prep required to start teaching.

Common uses: equipping a full classroom, after-school STEM clubs, makerspace lending libraries, and computer-science fairs.

products.name: CincoBit
products.tagline: A low-cost, micro:bit inspired educational board
products.description:
CincoBit is a low-cost, micro:bit-inspired board built around a 5×5 LED matrix, light and temperature sensors, two buttons, and an on-board buzzer. It uses the standard micro:bit edge connector with alligator-clip pads, plus a DUELink [Downlink](/docs/interface/downlink) port for chaining sensors, displays, and motors as projects grow.

Code it from a browser with [MicroBlocks](/docs/language/microblocks) — the easiest classroom workflow, with a complete [CS curriculum](/docs/tutorial/cincobit) included — or program it in [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, Arduino, or C++ once students are ready for typed code.

CincoBit is the flagship board behind the [Classroom CincoBit Kit](/docs/products/ktcincobit-e): 14 boards, USB cables, and storage for under $100.

:::tip
Microsoft MakeCode doesn't run on CincoBit directly; use MicroBlocks instead.
:::

products.name: PixoBit
products.tagline: micro:bit Form Factor with 128x64 OLED 0.96" Display
products.description:
PixoBit is a CincoBit-class educational board with a **128×64 OLED display** added on top of the same micro:bit edge connector, light and temperature sensors, two buttons, and on-board buzzer. The display lets students plot sensor data, draw graphics, or build games — anything where text alone isn't enough.

Code it from a browser with [MicroBlocks](/docs/language/microblocks) — same workflow as CincoBit — or program it in [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, Arduino, or C++ once students are ready for typed code. The [Downlink](/docs/interface/downlink) port chains additional modules as projects grow.

Pick PixoBit when projects need a graphical display. Otherwise [CincoBit](/docs/products/mccincobit-e) is the lower-cost starting point.

:::tip
Microsoft MakeCode doesn't run on PixoBit directly; use MicroBlocks instead.
:::

products.name: Clipit
products.description:
Clipit is an over-sized educational board with **alligator-clip pad holes** around the perimeter, capacitive touch pads, a 5×5 LED matrix, light sensor, and an on-board buzzer. The larger 120×60 mm footprint makes it easy to handle in event settings — STEM nights, demo booths, after-school clubs — and the clip holes let students wire to fruit, foil, conductive paint, or any other circuit they bring.

Clipit ships with [MicroBlocks](/docs/language/microblocks) firmware pre-loaded, so it works out of the box for block coding. Or program it in [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, Arduino, or C++. The [Downlink](/docs/interface/downlink) port chains more modules.

Pick Clipit for hands-on STEM activities where students will physically connect things to the board.

products.name: DueDuino
products.tagline: Arduino form factor inspired DUELink mainboard
products.description:
DueDuino is a DUELink microcomputer in the classic Arduino form factor — same shield-compatible pin layout, same mounting holes, USB-C, and a power jack. Drop in an Arduino shield and the board behaves like an STM32-based Arduino.

Program it directly in the [Arduino IDE](/docs/language/arduino-ide), or use it as a host that drives a chain of DUELink modules from [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, MicroPython, or scripting. The [Downlink](/docs/interface/downlink) port adds sensors, displays, and motors over a single cable without rewiring.

Pick DueDuino when your project — or your students — already work in the Arduino ecosystem.

products.name: DueSTEM
products.description:
DueSTEM is the most feature-packed board in the classroom range — a **128×64 OLED display, accelerometer, light and temperature sensors, four user buttons, two servo ports, RGB LED, and a buzzer** all on a single 107×57 mm board. Built around an "Input → Process → Output" model so students can demonstrate every step of physical computing on one piece of hardware.

Code it from a browser with [MicroBlocks](/docs/language/microblocks), or program it in [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, Arduino, or C++. The dedicated [DueSTEM tutorial](/docs/tutorial/duestem) walks through a complete first project. Extend further with female pin headers and the [Downlink](/docs/interface/downlink) port for chaining additional modules.

Pick DueSTEM for advanced classroom projects — games, sensor dashboards, motion-controlled demos — where you want everything on one board.

products.name: Stamp
products.tagline: Seeed Xiao Form Factor DUELink Microcomputer
products.description:
Stamp is a fingertip-sized DUELink microcomputer (24×18 mm) in the **Seeed Xiao form factor**. Plug it into a breadboard with male headers, surface-mount it using the edge pads, or drop it onto a Xiao-compatible carrier. An upgraded regulator lets it host a long chain of DUELink modules over its [Downlink](/docs/interface/downlink) port.

Program it in the [Arduino IDE](/docs/language/arduino-ide), bare-metal C++, or drive it as a host from [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, or MicroPython.

Pick Stamp for compact projects where you need USB on the board itself. If you don't need USB, [Stick](/docs/products/mcstick-a) is even smaller.

products.name: Stick
products.tagline: DIP24 Form Factor SMT DUELink SoM
products.description:
Stick is a surface-mount DUELink module in a **DIP24 footprint** (30.5×12.7 mm) — the smallest DUELink microcomputer, designed to be embedded in your own custom modules and products. Both Uplink and Downlink JST connectors are on board, and the USB pins are exposed for wiring out to a separate USB connector if you need one.

Program it in the [Arduino IDE](/docs/language/arduino-ide), bare-metal C++, or drive it as a host from [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, or MicroPython.

Pick Stick when you're designing your own hardware around DUELink. For breadboard prototyping with built-in USB, [Stamp](/docs/products/mcstamp-b) is the easier choice.

products.name: Ghizzy Jr
products.description:
Ghizzy Jr is a pocket-sized robot-shaped microcomputer that plugs directly into a USB port via PCB legs — no cable needed. Two RGB LEDs in the eyes and a [Downlink](/docs/interface/downlink) port for chaining additional DUELink modules make it a tiny but capable maker board.

Designed primarily as a branded give-away for events. The board is **special-order only, customized with your artwork**. Listed price is for an order of 500 pieces; quantities of 250 are available at a higher per-unit price, with a 4-week lead time. [Contact us](https://www.ghielectronics.com/contact/) for details.

Code it in [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, Arduino, or C++ — or run it standalone with [MicroBlocks](/docs/language/microblocks).

:::note
The "legs" are a printed circuit, not a standard USB connector. Works reliably on the vast majority of devices.
:::

products.name: Ghizzy
products.description:
Ghizzy is a robot-shaped DUELink microcomputer with two RGB eyes, ear and mouth LEDs, an on-board buzzer, two user buttons, and PCB USB-A legs that plug straight into a USB port. Both Uplink and Downlink JST connectors are on board, so Ghizzy can be hosted by another DUELink module or host a chain of its own.

Code it in [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, Arduino, or C++ — or run it standalone with [MicroBlocks](/docs/language/microblocks). The dedicated [Ghizzy tutorial](/docs/tutorial/ghizzy) walks through a complete first project.

Pick Ghizzy for character-driven STEM projects, interactive desk toys, or event give-aways with built-in personality.

:::note
The "legs" are a printed circuit, not a standard USB connector. Works reliably on the vast majority of devices.
:::

products.name: Holiday Tree
products.description:
Holiday Tree is a stand-up DUELink microcomputer in the shape of a Christmas tree — two PCBs (A and B) slide into each other for a 3D look. **26 individually addressable LEDs** and an on-board buzzer let you choreograph lights and sound however you want.

A USB cable is included, so the tree plugs directly into a computer or a phone charger and works out of the box. Code it in [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, Arduino, or C++, or run it standalone with [MicroBlocks](/docs/language/microblocks) — it ships pre-loaded with MicroBlocks firmware.

Pick Holiday Tree for seasonal projects students can take home, classroom decor that does something more than blink, or just a desk ornament with personality. Project assets at [holidaytree.duelink.com](https://holidaytree.duelink.com/).

Snowy is a snowflake-shaped DUELink module available in two versions: pre-populated with **12 surface-mount LEDs** for plug-and-play use, or unpopulated so students can solder their own LEDs as a learning exercise.

products.name: Chrono
products.description:
Chrono is a wearable-friendly DUELink microcomputer built around a **5×5 LED matrix**, two user buttons, and a power section that accepts both CR2032 coin cells and 3.7 V lithium batteries. Slots at the ends let it ride on a wristband or lanyard.

Code it in [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, Arduino, or C++, or run standalone with [MicroBlocks](/docs/language/microblocks).

Pick Chrono for wearable projects, name badges, scrolling-message demos, or any classroom activity where students want to take their work home.
keyFeatures: Slotted ends for wristband or mounting

products.name: Piano
products.description:
Piano is a DUELink microcomputer shaped like a piano keyboard — **capacitive touch pads laid out as keys**, multiple octaves, an on-board buzzer, and five status LEDs. Alligator-clip holes at the top let students play notes by touching fruit, foil, or other conductive objects.

Ships with [MicroBlocks](/docs/language/microblocks) firmware pre-loaded, so block coding works out of the box. Or code it in [Python](/docs/language/python), JavaScript, .NET, Arduino, or C++. Mounting holes on the body let you bolt on additional DUELink modules.

Pick Piano for music-themed STEM activities, accessibility projects, or any setting where the "input" needs to be something more tangible than a button press.

Controller is a 7-button DUELink microcomputer with an **8-LED ring** for status indication. Use it as a self-contained input board or as a controller layer for other DUELink modules over its [Downlink](/docs/interface/downlink) port.

products.name: Portal CP70
products.tagline: 7" - 800x400 Color Touch Display with Graphics Co-Processor
products.description:
A **7" 800×400 full-color touch display** with an on-board **BT817Q graphics coprocessor**. The coprocessor handles rendering — drawing primitives, font work, and image blits — so the host commands a high-level draw pipeline with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro), rather than pushing pixels itself.

Optional capacitive multi-touch is available, so the same module reads back finger positions for interactive UIs.

Common uses: industrial control panels, kiosk displays, point-of-sale interfaces, in-vehicle data screens, and custom embedded GUIs.

Because the BT817Q has its own command set, Portal CP70 doesn't use the engine's [Graphics](/docs/engine/graphics) library — see the Commands tab for the supported draw primitives.

products.name: ePaper M42
products.description:
A **4.2" three-color (red/white/black) e-Ink display** at 400×300 native resolution (200×150 effective with a 2× pixel multiplier). E-Ink draws power only while updating — once the image is set, you can disconnect power and the display holds the image indefinitely.

Render with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro), using the engine's [Graphics](/docs/engine/graphics) library.

Common uses: shelf labels, info badges, room signage, battery-powered weather displays, and any project where the screen is updated occasionally and runs for weeks on a coin cell.

products.name: HDMI
products.description:
Adds **HDMI video output** to any DUELink host. An on-board **BT817Q graphics coprocessor** with its own memory handles rendering — your host commands a high-level draw pipeline with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro), and the coprocessor pushes the result to any TV or monitor over HDMI.

Common uses: digital signage, classroom displays driven from a tiny host, game outputs on a big screen, and IoT dashboards on a wall-mounted TV.

Because the BT817Q has its own command set, HDMI doesn't use the engine's [Graphics](/docs/engine/graphics) library — see the Commands tab for the supported draw primitives.

products.name: Relay P16
products.description:
A **16-channel optically-isolated relay board** rated **10 A at 250 V** per channel. Optical isolation keeps the relay coils electrically separated from the DUELink bus, so a relay short or coil surge can't damage the rest of your chain. Switch any relay with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro).

Common uses: home automation panels, industrial-style control boards, lighting installations, lab-equipment interlocks, and AC-powered classroom demos where isolation is required.

Requires an external **12 V** power supply for the relay coils via the on-board terminal block.

:::danger AC mains hazard
Mains-voltage AC (110/240 V) can cause electric shock, fire, or death. Only wire live AC power if you are qualified to do so — otherwise have a licensed electrician make the connections, or use these relays only for low-voltage DC loads.
:::

products.name: RS485
products.description:
An **RS485 transceiver** for talking to industrial sensors, PLCs, motor drivers, and other electrically-noisy long-distance gear. The module handles the differential signaling, direction switching, and termination internally — your host code sends and receives serial frames with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro).
![RS485 analog input wired in](pathname:///img/rs485-ain.jpg)
Common uses: industrial sensor networks (Modbus-style), factory-floor integration, long-cable runs in noisy environments, building-automation projects, and classroom intro-to-industrial-protocols labs.

products.name: CAN
products.description:
A **CAN bus interface** supporting **CAN V2.0B** with both standard and extended arbitration IDs, plus remote frames. Used widely in automotive electronics, industrial machinery, and any system where multiple controllers share one bus. The module handles arbitration, framing, and CRC internally — your host code sends and receives CAN frames with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro).

Common uses: reading OBD-II diagnostics, vehicle hacking and instrumentation, robot drive-train integration, multi-controller industrial projects, and classroom intro-to-CAN labs.

products.name: DMX
products.description:
A **DMX512 stage lighting interface** for controlling theatrical lights, fog machines, motorized fixtures, and other DMX-compatible gear over a standard **XLR connector**. Drive up to **512 channels** with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro) — the module handles framing and timing on the DMX bus.
![DMX-controlled stage light](pathname:///img/dmx-light.jpg)
The module also works as a **DMX receiver** — drop a commercial DMX controller into your project and read its channel values, so a stage tech's familiar console can drive your DUELink project.
![Commercial DMX controller](pathname:///img/dmx-controller.jpg)
Common uses: live stage and event lighting, DJ lighting rigs, escape rooms, themed installations, and any project that needs to talk to or pretend to be DMX gear.

DMX module uses the [Standard Library](../engine/stdlib) DMX bus. No other drivers are needed.

products.name: Ethernet
products.description:
An **Ethernet network module** with a complete TCP/IP stack — TCP and UDP at 10/100 BASE-T over a standard RJ45 LAN connector. The module handles the network stack internally, so your host code opens sockets, reads, and writes with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro).

Common uses: local-network IoT projects, building automation on a wired LAN, controlled-intranet dashboards, classroom networking labs, and bridging DUELink modules into existing wired infrastructure.

:::warning No SSL/TLS support
This module does not support secure (encrypted) connections. It works well on trusted local networks and controlled intranets but will not reach modern HTTPS APIs or cloud services like AWS, GCP, or Azure that require TLS.
:::

products.name: USB Host
products.description:

Common uses: logging sensor data, storing configuration, loading assets at runtime, replaying melodies.
products.cmdNotes:
:::tip
This module has no commands of its own — file access is handled by the engine's built-in [File System](/docs/engine/filesystem) library. The same file functions (`FsMnt`, `FsOpen`, `FsRead`, `FsWrite`, etc.) work whether you have USB Host, SD Card, or MicroSD connected, so the same code works across all DUELink storage modules.
:::

products.name: SD Card

FAT16 and FAT32 with long-filename support are built in, so reading and writing files works with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro), no filesystem library to integrate.

Common uses: long-running datalogging, configuration files that survive power cycles, asset libraries (images, melodies, scripts), and any project where a host PC would be the awkward way to manage files.

For a smaller form factor, see [MicroSD](/docs/products/stmicrosd-a). For larger USB Mass Storage devices, see [USB Host](/docs/products/stusbhost-b).

:::tip
This module has no commands of its own — file access is handled by the engine's built-in [File System](/docs/engine/filesystem) library. The same file functions (`FsMnt`, `FsOpen`, `FsRead`, `FsWrite`, etc.) work whether you have USB Host, SD Card, or MicroSD connected, so the same code works across all DUELink storage modules.
:::

products.name: MicroSD
products.description:
A **microSD card slot** that plugs into any DUELink chain — same filesystem capabilities as the full-size [SD Card](/docs/products/stsdcard-b) module, but in a smaller 32×17 mm footprint. Read and write files with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro), using the engine's [File System](/docs/engine/filesystem) library. FAT16 and FAT32 with long-filename support are built in.

Common uses: compact dataloggers, wearable storage, drone or robot telemetry recording, classroom builds with limited board space, and any project where a microSD format makes more sense than a full SD.

products.name: Flash
products.description:
**16 MB of on-board flash storage** — no card to insert, no removable media, just non-volatile storage soldered to the module. Read or write any sector (or build your own simple file system on top) with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro).

Common uses: factory-defined configuration storage, lookup tables, firmware-side audio samples, calibration data that needs to survive power cycles, and any project where you want storage but don't want the failure mode of a removable card.

Flash exposes raw sector-level read/write rather than a FAT file system. For file-system access, see [USB Host](/docs/products/stusbhost-b), [SD Card](/docs/products/stsdcard-b), or [MicroSD](/docs/products/stmicrosd-a).

products.name: Wireless ESP32
products.description:
A **Wi-Fi + Bluetooth wireless module** built around an ESP32. Two ways to use it:

1. **As a wireless host** — program and command DUELink modules over BLE or Wi-Fi from a phone, laptop, or remote PC.
2. **As wireless access inside a Daisylink** — drop it into a chain to give your existing wired host a wireless reach to remote modules.

Either way, your host keeps sending simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro) — the wireless link is transparent. See the [Wireless interface](/docs/interface/wireless) page for the full picture.

Common uses: untethered prototypes, mobile-app control of physical projects, classroom labs where the host PC isn't next to the hardware, and IoT projects that need to bridge the JST world to the network.

products.name: Distance

The module sends a high-frequency chirp and times the echo internally — read the distance in centimeters with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro), no pulse-timing or echo math required.

Common uses: obstacle avoidance on a rover, proximity-triggered lights or sounds, level sensing for tanks, and interactive installations.

The on-board heater control circuit drives the sensor at the correct duty cycle, and the host reads concentration values with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro).

Common uses: CO alarms, breathalyzer demos, gas leak detection, fermentation monitoring, and air-quality experiments where you want to compare sensor types.

The MQ element is not auto-identified — recalibrate thresholds in your code after swapping.

products.name: Current

Built-in peripherals on the HAT — a **0.96" graphical OLED display**, two user buttons, and a piezo buzzer — drive directly from your Pi with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro). The OLED uses the engine's [Graphics](/docs/engine/graphics) library, so the display becomes part of your DUELink draw pipeline rather than a separate Linux framebuffer.

An on-board 800 mA regulator powers the Downlink chain independently, keeping load off the Pi's 3.3 V rail. A USB-C connector is available for firmware updates — leave it disconnected when the Pi is the host.

Pins on the **terminal block are DUELink GPIO**, not Pi GPIO — so they can handle real-time tasks like driving servo motors and addressable LEDs (WS2811/WS2812 strips) that are notoriously hard from a Raspberry Pi alone, where Linux's lack of real-time scheduling makes PWM jitter and timing glitches unavoidable without specialized hardware.

For a basic, lower-cost adapter without on-board peripherals, see [RPILink](/docs/products/adrpilink-a).

:::note
With the USB connector and the on-board regulator, DuePi can also run as a standalone microcomputer without a Pi attached.
:::
keyFeatures: Attaches to Raspberry Pi or Pi Zero Header

products.name: RPILink
products.description:
A **basic Raspberry Pi adapter** that adds a DUELink Downlink to any Pi model including Pi Zero — the simpler, lower-cost sibling to [DuePi](/docs/products/adduepi-b) (no OLED, no buttons, no buzzer). The top half plugs into the standard 40-pin RPi header; the bottom Holey section is a 5 mm-pitch grid that can mount DUELink modules directly, and is breakable for Pi Zero builds.

Power for chained modules comes from the Pi's internal regulators — fine for a few sensors and lights.

Common uses: minimal Pi-to-DUELink projects, classroom labs, low-budget builds, headless data-logging dongles, and anywhere you want JST Downlink on a Pi without paying for HAT-level peripherals.

:::tip
For heavier loads, add [Power Inject](/docs/products/adpwrinj-a) to your chain so relays, motors, or display modules don't pull through the Pi's rails.
:::
keyFeatures: Attaches to Raspberry Pi or Pi Zero Header

products.name: DuePico
products.description:
An **adapter for the Raspberry Pi Pico** that mounts the Pico on its back side and exposes every Pico pin on female headers. Built-in peripherals on the front — a **0.96" graphical OLED display**, two user buttons, and a piezo buzzer — drive directly with simple [text commands](?show=commands) over any supported [interface](/docs/interface/intro). The OLED uses the engine's [Graphics](/docs/engine/graphics) library.

An on-board 800 mA regulator powers the Downlink chain independently of the Pico's 3.3 V rail. A USB-C connector is available for firmware updates — leave it disconnected when the Pico is the host.

:::note
With the USB connector and the on-board regulator, DuePico can also run as a standalone microcomputer without a Pico attached.
:::
products.MfgNotes: Check Menu->Help for connection before testing

products.name: DueShield
products.description:
An **Arduino shield** with a DUELink I2C Downlink and a 5 mm-pitch Holey grid for mounting DUELink modules right on top of an Arduino UNO-compatible board. The grid lets you build a compact, mechanically tidy stack instead of a dangling cable nest.

Common uses: classroom Arduino + DUELink projects, custom Arduino-based instruments, hobby builds that need a polished enclosure, and any case where keeping the modules over the Arduino's footprint matters.

products.name: LinkBit
products.description:
A **micro:bit adapter** that brings a standard DUELink Downlink to the micro:bit edge connector. Plug a micro:bit in and any DUELink module can now ride downstream — same chain protocol, same text commands, no breakout wiring required.

Common uses: classroom MicroBlocks builds that grow beyond the micro:bit's on-board features, micro:bit + sensor/display projects, and integrating existing micro:bit code with DUELink modules.

products.name: DueMBUS
products.description:
A **MikroBUS Click host adapter** that lets a DUELink chain drive any MikroBUS Click module — thousands of pre-built sensors, comms modules, and actuators from the [MikroE catalog](https://www.mikroe.com/click). Also works as a basic female-header GPIO connector when you don't need the Click socket.

Common uses: drop-in adoption of the Click ecosystem from DUELink projects, classroom and hobby builds that mix Click and DUELink modules, and prototyping with parts you already own.

products.name: MBUSLink
products.tagline: Turn any MikroBUS Host into a DUELink Host
products.description:
Sits in a **MikroBUS socket** and exposes a DUELink [Downlink](/docs/interface/downlink) connector on the other side — so any SBC with a [MikroBUS](https://www.mikroe.com/mikrobus) host port can drive a DUELink chain. Pick **I2C** or **UART** for the bus.

Common uses: bridging existing MikroBUS-equipped SBCs (clusterboards, edge gateways, industrial Pi boards) into DUELink projects without changing the host.

products.name: Power Inject
products.description:
Injects **extra power into a long DUELink chain** — useful when downstream modules draw more than the main host can supply (servos, motors, large LED strips, multi-channel relays). Power comes in over USB-C or a terminal block; the regulated rail then feeds modules downstream of the inject point.

Common uses: long chains with heavy loads, mixed-current projects where some modules need quiet logic-level power and others need a beefier rail, and distributed installations where Power Inject sits closer to the demanding module than the host.
keyFeatures: USB Type C for Power Only

products.name: Power Switch
products.description:
A **physical toggle switch** that cuts power to its Downlink connector — flip it off and everything downstream is powered down. An on-board LED indicates the current state.

Common uses: bench-power kill switches, classroom safety cutoffs, sequenced power-on for delicate downstream modules, and any project where a tactile on/off feels safer than software-only control.

products.name: Power Hook
products.description:
Like a [USB Hook](/docs/products/adusbhook-a) but with an added **3.7 V 10440 lithium-ion battery charging circuit** — plug in a 10440 to run a DUELink chain untethered, then connect USB-C to recharge while still powering the chain. Battery is **required but not included**.

Common uses: portable demos, wearable prototypes, mobile dataloggers, take-anywhere projects that need to run for hours without finding an outlet.

:::danger AAA cells are not safe to use here
The 10440 is the same physical size as a AAA battery but is a **lithium-ion** cell — 3.7 V, lithium chemistry, designed for charging. Inserting a non-rechargeable AAA (1.5 V alkaline) or a NiMH AAA can cause the charging circuit to fail, leak, or in the worst case catch fire. **Only use 10440 cells.**
:::

products.name: USB Hook
products.description:
Adds **USB-C connectivity** to any DUELink module — plug in a USB-C cable to a PC, phone charger, or power bank and your module is on the bus. Includes an on-board 800 mA regulator and a power LED.

Common uses: connecting JST-only modules to a host PC for prototyping, powering small chains from a USB charger, classroom labs where every workbench has USB, and as the primary USB connection for any chain that doesn't have a microcomputer in it.

For USB Type-A (plug straight into a port without a cable), see [USB Stick](/docs/products/adusbstick-a). For battery-backed portability, see [Power Hook](/docs/products/adpwrhook-a).

products.name: USB Stick
products.description:
Adds **USB Type-A connectivity** to any DUELink module — plugs directly into a USB-A port on a PC, phone charger, or power bank, no cable required. Includes an on-board 800 mA regulator and a power LED.

Common uses: ultra-compact demo dongles, slot-into-laptop classroom projects, smallest-possible-footprint chains, and event give-aways where "just plug it in" matters.

For USB-C with a cable, see [USB Hook](/docs/products/adusbhook-a). For battery-backed portability, see [Power Hook](/docs/products/adpwrhook-a).

products.name: Sticker
products.description:
A **development board for the [Stick](/docs/products/mcstick-a) microcomputer** — exposes every Stick pin on a mix of male and female headers, with the male headers in a standard servo-style power + ground + signal pinout for easy prototyping. RST and LDR buttons on the board for resetting and forcing DFU mode during firmware development.

Common uses: prototyping with Stick before designing custom hardware around it, classroom labs on the Stick form factor, and bench testing during product development.

products.name: Breakout
products.description:
A **DUELink signal breakout** that exposes every JST cable signal on a female header — useful for wiring DUELink modules to a host board that doesn't have a JST connector (an Arduino, an Adafruit Feather, a custom PCB). Also works as a JST cable extender when you need extra length. Both the **DRP** and **CTN** pins carry a mix of [UART](/docs/interface/uart), [I2C](/docs/interface/i2c), and [USB](/docs/interface/usb) signals from the [interfaces](/docs/interface/intro) page — wire whichever matches what your host supports. Wiring examples:
![Arduino + Breakout, UART wiring](pathname:///img/arduino-breakout-uart.webp)
![Arduino + Breakout, I2C wiring](pathname:///img/arduino-breakout-i2c.webp)
Common uses: wiring DUELink to JST-less host boards, lengthening a chain when the cable is too short, classroom labs that show the protocol on a scope.

products.name: PullUp
products.description:
A tiny **inline 2.2 kΩ I2C pull-up module** for systems whose I2C host doesn't include its own pull-ups. Splices into the chain wherever pull-ups are needed; no commands, no configuration.

Common uses: bare microcontrollers used as DUELink hosts, custom PCBs missing pull-ups, classroom labs on signal integrity, and troubleshooting I2C reliability problems.

products.name: Cable
products.description:
**DUELink JST cables** in the lengths and quantities your projects need. Cables match the standard DUELink 4-pin 1 mm JST connector and plug into any module's Uplink or Downlink port.

Available packs:

- **Variety Pack** — 1× 300 mm, 2× 150 mm, 7× 50 mm (10 cables total)
- **5× 50 mm**
- **5× 150 mm**
- **5× 300 mm**
- **5× 100 mm JST-to-pin-header breakout** — for wiring DUELink modules to bare microcontroller pins

Modules ship without cables to reduce waste — pick a pack here for any chain you're building.

products.name: Connector
products.description:
A pack of **10× DUELink JST connectors** (4-pin, 1 mm pitch) — the same connectors used on every DUELink module's Uplink and Downlink ports. Available in **white** (Uplink color) and **blue** (Downlink color).

Common uses: building [your own DUELink modules](/docs/catalog/third-party), prototyping with the JST standard, repairs, custom adapters and breakouts.

:::note
The **blue** connectors are made for hand soldering and will discolor in a reflow oven. For volume production with reflow-compatible blue connectors, [contact us](https://www.ghielectronics.com/contact/).
:::

products.name: Holey Board
products.tagline: Holey Boards for Mounting DUELink Modules
products.description:
Blank circuit boards with **5 mm-pitch mounting holes** that exactly match the standard DUELink module footprint — so any module on a Holey Board mounts firmly with standoffs and screws instead of dangling at the end of a cable.

Available in four sizes:

- **Holey Stick** — 4 × 12 holes (22 × 62 mm)
- **Holey Smally** — 18 × 12 holes (92 × 62 mm)
- **Holey** — 36 × 18 holes (182 × 92 mm)
- **Holey Moley** — 46 × 36 holes (182 × 232 mm)

Common uses: tidying a final-build chain into a presentable layout, classroom projects students take home, mounting fixtures in enclosures, demos and trade-show setups.

See [Mounting Hardware](/docs/products/axmnt) for compatible standoffs, screws, nuts, and footpads.

products.name: Mounting HW
products.description:
**M3 mounting hardware** sized to match the DUELink Holey Boards — standoffs, screws, nuts, and footpads in nylon (with metal footpads). Universal M3 size also works with 3D-printed and laser-cut enclosures.

Available packs:

- **Variety Pack** — 20× M-F standoffs, 20× screws, 20× nuts, 4× footpads
- **10× male-to-female M3 nylon standoffs**
- **10× female-to-female M3 nylon standoffs**
- **10× M3 nylon screws**
- **10× M3 nylon nuts**
- **4× M3 metal footpads**

Common uses: building tidy final-form chains on a [Holey Board](/docs/products/axholey), fastening modules inside enclosures, classroom builds students can carry without losing modules.
